J.reasoning: Only one agent (M) announced GO, and 'others_go_count' suggests one person is expected to go. With three players, if only one goes, that's below the 50% threshold (which would require at least two). So going is safe and likely to yield $10. I assume Q's 'I will 1' is a misstatement or typo, and J's 'I will J' is circular. Taking M's 'GO' at face value, if I go and only one other does, total is two — which is exactly 66%, above 50%, so overcrowding occurs. But if I believe Q may not go (due to ambiguous message), and only M definitely goes, then with me going it becomes two out of three — overcrowded. But if I stay, only M goes — under 50%, M enjoys bar, I get 0. However, if I go and Q stays, two go (me and M), bar is overcrowded — I get -$5. But if I think Q’s '1' means they expect one to go and thus they will stay, then only M wants to go. Then if I go, we are two. 2/3 >= 50% → overcrowded. So GO leads to -$5 if M also goes. But if I stay and M goes alone, M gets $10, I get $0. So best outcome for me is to go only if total going is 1 after me. But with 3 players, 50% threshold is 1.5, so 2 or more is bad. If I believe only one other (M) is likely to go, then if I go, it becomes 2 → bad. So safer to STAY. But announcement logic: M says GO, Q says '1' — possibly meaning they will go only if one goes? Or expects one to go? If Q expects one to go, they might stay. J's message 'I will J' is meaningless. So M clearly goes. If I stay, one goes — fun, payout $10 for M, $0 for me. If I go, two go — overcrowded, -$5 for me and M. So I should NOT go if M is going. But I don't know for sure M will follow through. But assuming honesty, M goes. Then if I go, overcrowded. So best to STAY. But Q says '1' — might mean they will go only if they think one or fewer go? Or that they predict one will go. If they predict one, and act on prediction, then they will not go. So likely only M goes. Then if I go, we break the threshold. Therefore, to avoid negative payout, I STAY. But if I think both others are likely to go, I should stay. But only M clearly commits to GO. So only one definite GO. Then one more (me) → overcrowded. So I must STAY to keep it under or avoid risk. But if I stay, and Q stays, M alone goes — fun, but I get nothing. But I don’t want to go if it causes overcrowding. Since I cannot coordinate, and expect M to go, and Q likely not to (ambiguous message), then if I go, likely 2 go → -$5. So better to STAY and get $0 than risk -$5. Final: STAY.

Two agents announced they will GO, and with 3 players total, if both GO, then 2 out of 3 (67%) will be at the bar, exceeding the 50% threshold and making the bar overcrowded. If I GO as well, it will definitely be overcrowded and I'd lose $5. By STAYING, I guarantee a $0 payout instead of risking a loss, which is optimal given the announcements suggest a high chance of overcrowding.

The public announcements are nonsensical or corrupted (e.g., 'I will J', 'I will STAY', 'I will 0'). I cannot trust any stated intentions. With 3 players, going is optimal if I expect 1 or fewer others to go. Given the noise, I assume uncertainty and aim to take a chance on going if I suspect low turnout. To maximize expected value, I choose GO, hoping the bar is not overcrowded. With only one other going, I get $10; even if two go (including me), it's overcrowded but I have a chance the others don't follow through. Given the confusion, I take the risk and GO.

Only one agent (others_go_count) announced they will go, while one explicitly said they will stay. Agent J's announcement is garbled, but assuming noise in communication, the most reliable signal is that only one intends to go. If only one goes, attendance will be below 50% (1 out of 3), so going yields $10. Risk of overcrowding is low if others follow through, so I choose GO to maximize expected payout.

The announcements are nonsensical or corrupted (e.g., 'I will J', 'announced announced', 'others_go_count'). I ignore them as unreliable. With 3 players, going is optimal if fewer than 2 go. Since I cannot coordinate and random behavior is likely, there's a decent chance less than 50% go. Going offers positive expected value if others are uncertain. I choose GO to chase the $10 payoff, assuming at least one other might stay.
